planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in spring or fall; paperbark maple is slow-growing but tolerant of a wide range of soil conditions. It grows well in part shade, which can actually highlight the cinnamon-colored exfoliating bark better than full sun.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moist, slightly acidic soil in part shade to full sun; paperbark maple is widely considered to have the finest bark of any temperate deciduous tree — the rich cinnamon-orange to mahogany-red outer bark peels in papery, tissue-thin curling sheets year-round, revealing layers beneath in a constantly changing mosaic that glows in winter sunlight. The trifoliate leaves turn blazing red-orange in fall, making it a multi-season treasure. It grows slowly to 20–30 feet over 30–40 years, making it appropriate for smaller residential landscapes. Originally from central China, it has been widely planted in the United States since the early 1900s and is reliable in zones 4–8. The combination of exceptional bark, good fall color, and small stature makes it arguably the single best ornamental tree for the four-season garden where space is limited.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in spring or fall; trident maple is remarkably adaptable to urban conditions — compacted soils, air pollution, drought, heat, and alkaline soils — making it one of the most stress-tolerant small to medium deciduous trees available.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun to part shade; trident maple is a small to medium-sized Chinese maple that has become one of the most valued urban and street trees in temperate regions for its exceptional toughness, adaptability, and multi-season ornamental character. The distinctive three-lobed leaves resemble a trident in silhouette, turn brilliant orange-red to burgundy in fall, and the exfoliating orange-tan bark on mature specimens is decorative in winter. Trident maple is one of the most popular species for bonsai cultivation, where its small leaves, fine branching, and easily trained habit produce classical specimens. As a landscape tree it is tolerant of heat, drought, alkaline soil, and urban pollution far beyond most maples — making it the preferred maple for zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens where standard sugar maple and red maple often struggle in the heat.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in moist, well-drained, acid, humus-rich soil with excellent drainage; Franklinia demands perfect drainage and will not tolerate wet or compacted soils — root rot (Phytophthora) is the primary cause of failure in unsuitable sites. Plant in raised beds in heavy clay soils. All cultivated specimens worldwide descend from seeds collected by John Bartram in 1765.

directions: Plant in moist, acid, humus-rich, perfectly drained soil in part shade to full sun; Franklinia alatamaha is the most famous extinct-in-the-wild tree in North America — last seen growing wild along the Altamaha River in Georgia by John Bartram in 1803 and never found wild again, every Franklinia tree alive today descends from seeds or cuttings taken by Bartram before the species vanished from nature. The tree itself is extraordinary: large (3–4 inch) camellia-like white flowers with brilliant golden stamens bloom from August through October at the same time as the leaves turn flaming orange-red, producing a spectacular late-season display unlike any other temperate tree. The flowers are fragrant. It is a rare opportunity to grow a tree that would be extinct today without human cultivation — both a horticultural treasure and a living piece of botanical history.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil; hybrid witch-hazel is best sited where it can be seen and appreciated from inside the house during its January–March bloom period. It grows well in part shade and is excellent near the edge of a woodland garden.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in part shade to full sun; hybrid witch-hazel is a cross between Japanese witch-hazel (Hamamelis japonica) and Chinese witch-hazel (H. mollis) and includes the most spectacular named garden cultivars of any witch-hazel — producing their strap-petaled, spidery flowers in January and February directly on leafless branches, providing color at the darkest time of year when almost nothing else is blooming. The flowers are heavily and sweetly fragrant and can be enjoyed cut indoors. Outstanding named cultivars include 'Arnold Promise' (clear yellow, most cold-hardy), 'Jelena' (luminous copper-orange), 'Diane' (deep red to garnet), and 'Ruby Glow' (copper-red). Fall foliage is outstanding in orange, red, and yellow. Hybrid witch-hazels are larger and more vigorous than either parent, eventually forming large multi-stem shrubs or small trees to 15 feet across.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in moist, well-drained soil in spring or fall; Shantung maple is notably tolerant of drought, alkaline soils, heat, and urban conditions — making it far more adaptable to the South and Southwest than its more common relatives. The 'Norwegian Sunset' and 'Pacific Sunset' cultivars are common hybrid selections (A. truncatum x A. platanoides).

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral or even alkaline soil in full sun to part shade; Shantung maple is a medium-sized Chinese maple of outstanding toughness and ornamental value — the young spring leaves emerge in shades of rich purple-burgundy before maturing to glossy, deep green, and the fall color is consistently a rich orange-red, comparable to red maple in intensity. It grows 20–30 feet with an upright-oval to rounded crown and is among the most heat-tolerant and drought-tolerant maples available, thriving in the hot, dry, alkaline conditions of the Great Plains and Southwest where most other maples fail. Unlike Norway maple, it is non-invasive and better-behaved in the landscape. 'Norwegian Sunset' and 'Pacific Sunset' (A. truncatum x platanoides hybrids) have been widely planted in street tree programs across the western and central United States for their exceptional adaptability.
